claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 16 and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 16, I need to ask: Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as school, college, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They left school recently.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has left education or training, I have a follow-up question:

Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services? (If so, you may still be able to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask a few more questions about Alex's current situation:

Is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question about Alex:

Is Alex currently claiming any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't claiming any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also confirm: Do you live in the UK, and are there any other children you're responsible for that you'd like me to assess for Child Benefit eligibility?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester actually. And no, Alex is my only child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit can continue if the child is under 20 **and** they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex has left education and is not in any approved training, the standard eligibility does not apply.

**However, there may be an option available to you:**

If Alex registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you could claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks. This would give Alex some time to find their next steps while you continue receiving support.

I'd recommend encouraging Alex to register with a careers service as soon as possible if you'd like to pursue this option. If Alex does register, you should contact HMRC's Child Benefit Office to inform them and potentially extend your eligibility for those 20 weeks.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you'll still qualify if they are under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Alex has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks after leaving education). Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week and is not claiming benefits in their own right. However, since Alex has left education/training and has not registered with the careers service or armed services, and is not in approved education or training, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with them. While Alex is not working or claiming benefits, they have left education and are not in approved training, nor have they registered with a careers service or the armed services. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for 16-17 year olds requires them to be in approved education or training, or to have registered with a careers service/armed services (for a 20-week period). Since none of these conditions are met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week and is not claiming benefits in their own right. However, since Alex has left education/training and has not registered with the careers service or armed services, and is not in approved education or training, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

While Alex is not working or claiming benefits, they have left education and are not in approved training, nor have they registered with a careers service or the armed services. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility for 16-17 year olds requires them to be in approved education or training, or to have registered with a careers service/armed services (for a 20-week period). Since none of these conditions are met, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
